item.title: Run Google Chrome as non root user in Kali Linux
item.description: This post is a part of the Project Restore, with the primary aim of excavating my previous writings. The material contained herein may not hold practical relevance and is most likely completely useless. This post is about running Google’s Chrome browser in Kali linux as a no-root user. Before I get into it, I would like to say that you can run Chrome as root and there are plenty of tutorials showing you how to do that.

item.title: Connect to ssh server on Kali Linux
item.description: For some of my work I run Kali in Virtual Box and I was getting a bit tired of switching between different windows on my mac host. So I thought I will ssh into my Kali box and enjoy the iTerm eye-candy. The version of Kali I have running which is 1.07 has openssh server installed but the service is not running by default. To run the service I ran this command

item.title: Linux - Setgid on Directory and Share Directory With Users
item.description: This post is a part of the Project Restore, with the primary aim of excavating my previous writings. The material contained herein may not hold practical relevance and is most likely completely useless. I came across a scenario which warranted using setgid. The requirement was to allow multiple users to create files and read files in a directory. I also wanted to ensure that this requirement can be easily met for any future users.

item.title: Linux - Number of Files in Directory
item.description: This post is a part of the Project Restore, with the primary aim of excavating my previous writings. The material contained herein may not hold practical relevance and is most likely completely useless. The command to get the number of files in a directory is.ls –l | grep ^- | wc –lIf you are here just to get the command then no need to read further. However if you’d like to understand a bit more then read on.
